---
title: "Developer docs"
description: "Working on EpiAware packages and this website."
---

Reference notes for developing EpiAware packages. Start with
[Contributing](contributing.qmd) for the overall workflow, and each package's
own developer documentation for specifics.

## Faster test runs

Quality checks (Aqua.jl, formatting, doctests) are the slowest part of a test
run. Skip them while iterating:

```bash
julia --project=test test/runtests.jl skip_quality
```

Use [Revise.jl](https://github.com/timholy/Revise.jl) so code changes take
effect without restarting Julia.

## Running only the tests you care about

Tests are written as `@testitem`s, so they can be run by path or by tag with
[TestItemRunner](https://github.com/julia-vscode/TestItemRunner.jl).

```julia
using TestItemRunner

run_tests("test/censoring/")                  # one directory
run_tests(filter = ti -> (:unit in ti.tags))  # one tag
```

The VS Code Julia extension exposes the same test items in its test explorer.

## Code quality

Packages run a consistent set of quality tools:

- [Aqua.jl](https://github.com/JuliaTesting/Aqua.jl) for common package problems
- [JET.jl](https://github.com/aviatesk/JET.jl) for static analysis (`@report_opt`, `@report_package`)
- Doctests and formatting checks

[EpiAwarePackageTools.jl](https://epiawarepackagetools.epiaware.org/) provides the shared
harnesses, including automatic-differentiation tests across backends.

## Checking performance

Each package has a benchmark suite, and most publish a benchmark page in their
docs showing which automatic-differentiation backends work, how fast they are,
and where they fail.

```bash
julia --project=benchmark benchmark/runbenchmarks.jl
```

For a single function, `BenchmarkTools` is usually enough.

```julia
using BenchmarkTools
@benchmark cdf(d, 5.0)
```

## Building documentation locally

Each package builds its own docs from `docs/`:

```bash
julia --project=docs docs/make.jl
```

Literate tutorials are the slow part of a docs build. Skip them for a quick
check:

```bash
julia --project=docs docs/make.jl --skip-notebooks
SKIP_NOTEBOOKS=true julia --project=docs docs/make.jl   # same thing
```

## This website

This site is built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org/). To preview locally,
install Quarto and run:

```bash
quarto preview      # live preview
quarto render       # build the static site into _site/
```

Every push and pull request builds the site in CI, so a broken build is caught
before merge. Pushes to `main` also deploy it to GitHub Pages. The workflow is
in `.github/workflows/publish.yml`.

To add a package to the [Packages](packages/index.qmd) list, add a row to the
table in `packages/index.qmd`. Give the row a `data-category` so it picks up the
category filter, link the package name to its documentation, and link the icon
to its source.

## When something breaks

**Your changes are not taking effect.**
Load Revise before the package, or restart Julia.

**"Package not found" while developing.**
Activate the right environment and instantiate it. If you are working on more
than one EpiAware package at once, `Pkg.develop` the ones you are changing so
the local copies are used rather than released versions.

**Precompilation errors.**
Run `] resolve`, then `Pkg.precompile()`. If it persists, delete
`Manifest.toml` and `] instantiate` to rebuild the environment.

More detail lives in each package's own developer FAQ.

## Releases

Releases are cut from `main` by tagging a version, following each package's
documented release process.
